title: What to know before using Angelica dahurica (Bai Zhi)?
intro_desc: Angelica dahurica, also known as Chinese Angelica or Dong Quai, is a herbaceous plant native to Northeast Asia. It has a long history of use in traditional Chinese medicine for treating various ailments such as headaches, inflammation, and respiratory infections. This plant thrives in cooler climates and well-drained soil, often found in mountainous regions. A. dahurica is characterized by its tall, sturdy stems and large, compound leaves, as well as its small, yellowish-white flowers that bloom in umbels. Its medicinal properties are attributed to its roots, which contain various bioactive compounds including essential oils, coumarins, and furanocoumarins, known for their anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antibacterial effects.
medicine_desc: Angelica dahurica has several medicinal uses, such as alleviating headaches, reducing inflammation, and relieving joint pain. Its constituents include essential oils, coumarins, and organic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. The root of the plant is typically prepared as a decoction, powder, or tincture in traditional Chinese medicine. However, Angelica dahurica may cause skin irritation and should be used with caution. Additionally, it should not be taken by pregnant women or those with a history of photosensitivity, as it may increase the risk of photodermatitis.
horticulture_desc: Angelica dahurica, also known as Dahurian Angelica, is a robust and hardy plant that thrives in cooler climates. It prefers partial shade to full sun and well-drained soil. This plant can grow up to 6 feet tall and 3 feet wide, so it requires ample space to spread its roots and branches. It is native to Northeastern Asia, particularly in China, Korea, and Russia, and can tolerate temperatures as low as -20°F. However, it is crucial to protect the plant from extreme heat and dryness during the summer months.

When planting Angelica dahurica, it is recommended to start the seeds indoors about 8-10 weeks before the last expected frost. The seeds require a period of cold stratification to break their dormancy, so it is advisable to refrigerate them for 2-3 weeks before sowing. Once the seedlings have grown to about 2-3 inches tall, they can be transplanted outdoors in a location with the appropriate sunlight and soil conditions. It is essential to space the plants at least 2 feet apart to allow for proper growth and air circulation.

Angelica dahurica requires regular watering and fertilization to maintain its health and vigor. It is recommended to water the plant deeply once a week, providing enough moisture to penetrate the root zone. However, it is crucial to avoid overwatering, as this can lead to root rot and other fungal diseases. A balanced fertilizer can be applied in the spring, following the package instructions, to provide the necessary nutrients for growth. It is also beneficial to mulch around the base of the plant to retain moisture and suppress weeds.

Harvesting Angelica dahurica can be done in the second year of growth, once the plant has established a strong root system. The roots are typically harvested in the fall, after the first frost, when the plant's medicinal properties are at their peak. To harvest, carefully dig around the base of the plant, taking care not to damage the roots. After harvesting, the roots should be washed thoroughly and cut into small pieces before drying. The leaves and stems can also be harvested in the summer, when they are at their most aromatic.

Angelica dahurica is relatively resistant to pests and diseases, but it can be susceptible to aphids, spider mites, and powdery mildew. To prevent infestations, it is recommended to keep the plant healthy and well-maintained, providing adequate water and nutrients. If pests are present, a natural insecticidal soap can be used to control the population. To prevent powdery mildew, it is essential to avoid overcrowding the plants and providing good air circulation. Regularly inspecting the plant and removing any affected leaves or stems can also help prevent the spread of the disease.
botany_desc: Angelica dahurica, also known as Chinese Angelica or Dong Quai, is a plant species belonging to the family Apiaceae, which includes carrots, parsley, and celery. It is a biennial or perennial herb that can grow up to 2 meters tall. Its taxonomy is well-established, with its scientific name, Angelica dahurica, given by the German botanist, Friedrich Ernst Ludwig Fischer in 1806. It is classified under the genus Angelica, which contains around 60 species, mostly from temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere.

Angelica dahurica has a distinctive morphology that makes it easily recognizable. It has a thick, stout stem that is hollow and grooved. The leaves are large, compound, and bi- or tripinnate, with a sheathing base. The flowers are small, white or greenish-white, and are arranged in large, compound umbels. The fruit is a dry, two-parted schizocarp, which splits into two mericarps at maturity. The roots are thick, fleshy, and aromatic, with a strong, characteristic odor.

Angelica dahurica has several variant names, including A. dahurica var. formosana, A. dahurica var. urticifolia, and A. dahurica var. sinensis. These variants differ in their morphological and anatomical characteristics, such as leaf shape, flower size, and fruit structure. For example, A. dahurica var. formosana has larger leaves and flowers than A. dahurica var. dahurica, while A. dahurica var. urticifolia has a more hairy stem and leaves. However, these variants are not always clearly distinguished, and some botanists consider them as part of a single, variable species.

Angelica dahurica is native to Northeast Asia, including China, Korea, Japan, and Russia. It prefers moist, shady habitats, such as forests, meadows, and riverbanks. It is commonly found in the mountainous regions of China, such as the provinces of Heilongjiang, Jilin, Liaoning, and Inner Mongolia. It is also cultivated in other parts of China, such as the Yangtze River Delta and the Pearl River Delta, for its medicinal and culinary uses. In Korea, it is found in the northern provinces of Hamgyeongnam-do and Kangwon-do, while in Japan, it is distributed in the northern island of Hokkaido and the northern part of Honshu.

Angelica dahurica has a biennial or perennial life-cycle, depending on the environmental conditions. In its first year, it produces a rosette of leaves and stores energy in its thick roots. In its second year, it bolts and produces a tall stem, flowers, and fruits. The seeds are dispersed by wind and water, and can remain viable in the soil for several years. The plant also reproduces vegetatively, through its rhizomes, which can produce new shoots and form colonies. The roots and rhizomes are harvested in the autumn of the second year, or in the spring of the third year, when the active constituents, such as essential oils, coumarins, and polysaccharides, are at their highest levels.
intro_desc_desc: Angelica dahurica, also known as Chinese Angelica or Dong Quai, is a herbaceous perennial plant native to Northeast Asia. It is a valuable medicinal plant, with roots that contain various compounds used in traditional medicine. These compounds include coumarins, ferulic acid, and phthalides, which have been found to have anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antibacterial properties. In horticulture, Angelica dahurica prefers partial shade and well-drained soil, and it can grow up to 2 meters tall with large, lobed leaves and umbels of white or pale pink flowers. The plant has a long history of use in traditional Chinese medicine, where it is often used to treat a variety of ailments, including headaches, menstrual cramps, and skin conditions.
benefits_desc: Angelica dahurica has many health benefits, such as its ability to alleviate headaches and migraines. Its anti-inflammatory properties can help reduce swelling and pain in the body, making it a popular natural remedy for conditions like arthritis and joint pain. Additionally, Angelica dahurica has been shown to boost the immune system, helping the body fight off infections and illnesses. Furthermore, it has been used in traditional medicine to improve skin conditions, such as acne and eczema, due to its antibacterial and soothing effects. Lastly, Angelica dahurica may also help improve digestion and alleviate symptoms of digestive disorders, such as bloating and indigestion.
constituents_desc: Angelica dahurica has many active constituents, such as essential oils, coumarins, and organic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The essential oils, including limonene, α- and β-phellandrene, and β-bisabolene, are known to have anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antibacterial effects. Coumarins, such as osthol and imperatorin, exhibit anti-coagulant, anti-tumor, and neuroprotective activities. Organic acids, such as ferulic and isoferulic acids, have been found to possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-tumor properties. These constituents work synergistically to make Angelica dahurica a valuable herb in traditional Chinese medicine for treating conditions such as headaches, toothaches, and inflammation. However, it is important to note that the use of Angelica dahurica should be under the guidance of a healthcare professional due to potential interactions and side effects.
preparations_desc: Angelica dahurica has many medicinal preparations, such as powders, decoctions, and extracts, which are used to treat various medical conditions. The dried root and rhizome of the plant are primarily used to create these preparations. A common use of these preparations is to alleviate headaches, including migraines and headaches caused by sinus issues. Additionally, they are used to treat inflammation, both externally and internally, and can be applied to the skin to help heal wounds and sores. Furthermore, Angelica dahurica preparations are also used in traditional Chinese medicine to address toothaches and other dental issues.
side_effects_desc: Angelica dahurica can have some side effects if used improperly, such as skin irritation and sensitivity to sunlight in some individuals. It may also cause digestive issues like stomach upset, nausea, and diarrhea when taken in large amounts or for extended periods. In addition, it can lead to allergic reactions, manifesting as rashes, itching, or difficulty breathing, especially in people with allergies to other members of the Apiaceae family. Lastly, inappropriate use of Angelica dahurica can potentially affect the menstrual cycle in women, causing changes in menstrual frequency or irregularities.
precautions_desc: You should take some precautions when using Angelica dahurica for medicinal purposes, such as carefully controlling the dosage and duration of use. It is essential to consult with a healthcare professional or herbalist to determine the appropriate dosage, as consuming too much can lead to adverse effects. Moreover, long-term use of this herb should be avoided, and it is recommended to take breaks in between usage to prevent potential toxicity. Pregnant and breastfeeding women should also avoid using Angelica dahurica, as its safety during pregnancy and lactation has not been established. Additionally, individuals with bleeding disorders or those taking anticoagulant medications should exercise caution when using this herb, as it may increase the risk of bleeding.
history_desc: Angelica dahurica, also known as Chinese Angelica or Dong Quai, has a long history of use in traditional medicine across Asia. This herb, which belongs to the Apiaceae family, has been used for thousands of years in China, Korea, and Japan to treat a wide range of ailments. Its roots and rhizomes are harvested, dried, and then prepared as decoctions, powders, or tinctures. Historically, Angelica dahurica has been used to alleviate various health issues, such as menstrual cramps, headaches, inflammation, and skin conditions. Its medicinal properties are attributed to the presence of essential oils, coumarins, and other bioactive compounds, which contribute to its anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antispasmodic effects.

In Chinese mythology, Angelica dahurica is deeply rooted in folklore and traditional beliefs. It is considered a potent symbol of femininity, fertility, and protection. According to legend, Angelica dahurica was first discovered by a divine herbalist who revealed its healing properties to humans. The plant is often associated with the goddess of mercy, Guanyin, who is believed to have bestowed the knowledge of its medicinal uses upon humanity. Furthermore, Angelica dahurica is considered a guardian plant that wards off evil spirits and protects against misfortune. Its spiritual significance is reflected in its use in various rituals and ceremonies, where it is often burned as incense or incorporated into amulets and talismans.

Throughout history, Angelica dahurica has played a significant role in various rituals and ceremonies, particularly in connection with spiritual protection, healing, and fertility. In ancient China, it was customary to burn Angelica dahurica as incense during religious ceremonies and important life events, such as weddings and funerals. Its fragrant smoke was believed to purify the air, dispel negative energy, and create a harmonious atmosphere. Additionally, Angelica dahurica was often used in fertility rituals, where it was thought to promote conception and ensure a healthy pregnancy. Women would carry dried Angelica dahurica roots in small pouches or wear them as necklaces to enhance their fertility and protect their unborn children.

Angelica dahurica has been documented in numerous ancient texts and literary works throughout history. Its medicinal properties and spiritual significance have been described in various classical Chinese medical texts, such as the "Compendium of Materia Medica" (Bencao Gangmu) and the "Divine Farmer's Materia Medica" (Shennong Bencaojing). These texts provide valuable insights into the historical understanding and application of Angelica dahurica in traditional Chinese medicine. Furthermore, its symbolism and mythological associations have been explored in various Chinese literary works, including poetry, novels, and folklore. These literary references not only reflect the cultural importance of Angelica dahurica but also contribute to its enduring legacy as a powerful and versatile plant.

history_intro_desc: Angelica dahurica has several historical medicinal uses, such as treating various ailments including headaches, inflammation, and respiratory infections. According to mythology, it is believed that the plant has the power to ward off evil spirits and protect against negative energy. Ancient rituals involving Angelica dahurica include burning it as incense during spiritual ceremonies to cleanse the environment and promote healing. In literature, the plant is often mentioned in traditional Chinese medicine texts, where it is praised for its medicinal properties and versatility. Symbolically, Angelica dahurica represents strength, resilience, and protection, making it a powerful tool in both physical and spiritual healing practices.
botany_intro_desc: Angelica dahurica belongs to the Apiaceae family, with the genus Angelica. This herbaceous perennial plant is characterized by its tall, sturdy stems and large, compound leaves. The plant can grow up to 2 meters tall and has white or pale pink flowers arranged in umbels. Variants of Angelica dahurica include A. dahurica var. formosana and A. dahurica var. erythropoda, which have different leaf shapes and flower colors. Angelica dahurica is native to Northeastern China, Mongolia, and the Korean peninsula, typically found in grasslands, scrubs, and forest edges. The plant undergoes a biennial life cycle, with a rosette of leaves in the first year and flowering in the second year.
